title: Git Insights Ministry
subtitle: From knowing the estate to rebuilding it: synthesizing thousands of findings into one modular, government-owned architecture.
abstract: Git Insights told us the truth about every repository, one at a time. But knowing what is wrong is not the same as knowing how to rebuild. Git Insights Ministry is the second lens. It reads a whole ministry of code at once, around 200 repositories, and works out everything those systems actually do. Then AI proposes a way to rebuild these capabilities as modern, government-owned modules, each built once, with nothing duplicated. In one Alberta ministry, 185 applications can be collapsed into 16 reusable modules. This paper shows how the tool works, what it gives a decision-maker, and how the shared architecture of government gets rebuilt.

hero_image.alt: A chaotic sprawl of mismatched old buildings being reorganized into a small set of clean, identical modern modules on a shared foundation.
hero_image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a chaotic sprawl of mismatched old buildings on one side being reorganized into a small set of clean, identical modern modular buildings on the other, all resting on one shared foundation. A sense of hundreds of redundant structures becoming a few reusable ones. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

slides.title: Git Insights Ministry
slides.caption: Paper 4 · Git Insights Ministry
slides.subcaption: From knowing the estate to rebuilding it.
slides.text: Git Insights Ministry. Git Insights told us the truth about every repository, one at a time. But knowing what is wrong is not the same as knowing how to rebuild. This is the second lens. It reads a whole ministry of code at once and works out a way to rebuild it, as modern, government-owned modules, each built once, with nothing duplicated.

slides.title: A whole ministry, read at once
slides.caption: The deeper read

visual_config.stat_label: Repositories in one ministry, read together

slides.subcaption: Some ministries run on hundreds of applications. The tool reads them as one body of work.
slides.text: The deeper read. Some ministries run on hundreds of applications. Git Insights Ministry pulls them all down, around two hundred repositories, and reads them as a single body of work. That is what makes the patterns visible, like the same login built forty times, or the same report written a dozen ways.

slides.title: A buildable record of every system
slides.caption: What it reconstructs

items.label: Screens and workflows
items.desc: Every page, and the paths a user takes between them.

items.label: APIs and data
items.desc: Every endpoint, and every table and field in the database.

items.label: Business rules
items.desc: Every policy, validation, and calculation, in plain English.

items.label: Integrations
items.desc: Every other system it talks to, and how critical each one is.

slides.text: For every system, the tool reverse-engineers what it does. Every screen and workflow, every API, every table in the database, every business rule, and every connection to another system. It writes all of this into a dossier detailed enough to rebuild the system from scratch, and every finding points back to the exact file it came from.

slides.title: Counted by code, judged by AI
slides.caption: How it works, and why you can trust it

left.label: Deterministic code
left.body: Clones the repositories and counts what is there. Exact, and cannot invent a result.

right.label: AI judgment
right.body: Groups the findings into capabilities and designs the modules, always tied back to a source file.

slides.text: How it works, and why you can trust it. The exact, countable work, cloning the code and counting what is there, is done by ordinary code that cannot invent a result. The AI is only asked for judgment, grouping the findings into capabilities and designing the modules that deliver them. Every conclusion it reaches can be traced back to real code.

slides.title: 185 applications into 16 modules
slides.caption: The synthesis
slides.subcaption: The redundancy the tool measured is the redundancy it designs out of the rebuild.
slides.text: The synthesis. The tool groups everything the ministry does into a small set of real capabilities, then designs modern modules to deliver them, each built once. In one Alberta ministry, one hundred and eighty-five applications collapsed into sixteen reusable modules. The redundancy the tool measured across the portfolio is the redundancy it designs out of the rebuild.

slides.title: Build once, use across government
slides.caption: Patterns of reuse

items.label: Payments
items.desc: Almost every ministry takes a payment. Build it once, securely.

items.label: Geospatial mapping
items.desc: Recurs across transport, environment, and land.

items.label: Contract management
items.desc: A back-office need every ministry solves on its own today.

items.label: Identity and access
items.desc: One sign-on, in place of a login rebuilt in every app.

slides.text: The most valuable patterns are the ones that repeat. Payments, mapping, contract management, a sign-on screen. Almost every ministry needs them, and today almost every ministry builds its own. We build them once, as shared services any ministry can use, and the complexity and attack surface of our infrastructure falls by more than ninety percent. The next paper shows the strategies for carrying out the rebuild.

sections.title: The synthesis vision: a government rebuilt

blocks.text: Git Insights offered the first step, by analyzing each repository and each application and giving us actionable intelligence. Not all of the insights were welcome or easy to review. The estate needed remediation, and with those findings in hand we have and continue to fix the most critical issues. But knowing is only the first part. We cannot always right the ship of government one plank at a time. Sometimes we need a whole new vessel.

Where [Git Insights](#/paper/bbkac) reads one repository at a time and reports its health, its sibling process Git Insights Ministry (GIM) reads an entire ministry of application code at once and synthesizes the findings into a plan. It looks for the patterns that only appear across a whole portfolio such as the same login component built forty times or the same report written in a dozen ways. From those patterns it proposes a modular, reusable, government-owned compute architecture to replace the sprawl.

blocks.label: Repositories read together, per ministry
blocks.body: Some ministries are enabled by hundreds of applications and repositories. Git Insights Ministry pulls them all down and analyzes them as one body of work, which is what makes the patterns visible.

blocks.text: The analysis goes far deeper than a health score. GIM clones every repository and reverse-engineers each capability. Every screen, workflow, API integration and endpoint, database schema and table, business rule, and dependency is read and recorded. From that raw material, this tool builds a dossier for each system, detailed enough to rebuild these functions anew.

blocks.title: What Git Insights Ministry reconstructs from each repository at the organizational level.
rows: A plain-language account of what the system is, who uses it, and how it is built.
Every screen and page, and the workflows that move a user between them.
Every endpoint, service, and batch job, with its inputs and outputs.
Every entity, table, view, and stored procedure, with fields and relationships.
Every policy, validation, and calculation, in plain English, with the exact file and line as evidence.
Every external system it talks to, the direction, the mechanism, and how critical it is.
What the system publishes, and what it listens for.
What the AI could not resolve from the code, and a handover-readiness rating.
blocks.caption:
A buildable record for every repository, grounded in evidence. 
Each finding cites the exact file it came from, so any claim can be checked against the code.

blocks.text: GIM runs in stages. Some are deterministic, analysis steps done by ordinary code that counts, clones, calculates, and cannot invent a result. Such scripts are fast and powerful but prone to false positives. The next steps are agentic, where AI is asked to apply judgment. The formats of its answers are strictly controlled and always tied back to evidence in the code. Every conclusion can be traced to the file it came from.

blocks.title: How GIM builds the new architecture
blocks.caption: The synthesis runs in stages: read every repository, cluster the findings into the things the ministry actually does, design the modules that deliver them once each, then specify and cost the rebuild.

steps.title: Reverse-engineer each function
steps.desc: AI reconstructs every screen, workflow, API, data model, rule, and integration into a handover-quality dossier, each finding tied to its source file.

steps.title: Cluster into business capabilities
steps.desc: AI groups the findings from all the repositories into the distinct things the ministry actually does for citizens. This business capability map becomes the basis for remediation.

steps.title: Design the target modules
steps.desc: AI proposes a small set of modern modules that deliver those capabilities just once, with no gaps or overlaps. A clear spec is built which can be vetted by human subject matter experts.

steps.title: Write a buildable spec per module
steps.desc: AI produces a rebuild specification for each module, its screens, APIs, data, rules, and integrations, with an audit that no capability is lost.

steps.title: Roll up to a costed ministry plan
steps.desc: Code computes the effort and a phased roadmap; AI writes the executive report. The plan is ready for approval, the architecture ready for orchestration and a first build.

blocks.text: The consolidation plan is created in two steps. First the tool groups the findings from every repository into the real things the ministry does for people. Then it designs the modules that deliver them, keeping separate work in separate modules and pulling anything common into the shared platform, so it is built once rather than forty times.

We do not ask anyone to take this on trust. Every capability the tool names points back to the systems and the workflows it came from, so we can always trace a conclusion to real code. Costing for the replacement systems are based on real heuristics, such as the number of screens, endpoints, database tables, and workflows. Ordinary code works it out from a fixed set of rates, so we can defend it line by line. The models themselves run on Claude through Google Vertex, working in parallel, one repository or one module at a time.

blocks.title: The synthesis vision: a government rebuilt

blocks.text: From the raw reconstruction of every system, it clusters every functional piece into the set of business capabilities the ministry genuinely performs, then designs a target architecture that delivers those capabilities as clean modules, with zero redundancy and zero overlap. Hundreds of applications, each carrying its own login, its own file upload, its own reporting, collapse into a handful of modules where each capability is built once.

blocks.label: One ministry, after synthesis
blocks.body: In one instance, 185 applications collapsed into 16 distinct modules. The redundancy the tool measured across the portfolio is the redundancy it designs out of the rebuild.

blocks.title: Many tangled applications, a few clean modules
blocks.caption: The consolidation in one image: a dense field of overlapping legacy applications resolves into a small set of distinct modules, each capability built once and shared rather than rebuilt in every system.

image.alt: A dense tangle of many small overlapping application boxes funneling down into a small set of clean, distinct modules.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a dense tangle of nearly two hundred small overlapping application boxes on one side funneling down into about sixteen clean, distinct, evenly-spaced modules on the other. A sense of redundancy collapsing into order. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.text: Every new module is built on one modern stack. That matters. It means we are no longer locked into a vendor we cannot leave. Our attack surface is minimized. Each module is based on open-source technologies and expanded to meet our specific policies. The staff and agents which build these systems retain the knowledge to maintain them.

The most valuable patterns are the ones that repeat. Across ministries, the same capabilities appear again and again. Several of the modules from a single ministry, in this case payments, geospatial mapping, and contract management, are not unique at all. Rather than rebuild them inside every ministry, we are building them once, as shared services that any ministry can use.

blocks.title: Build once, use across government
blocks.caption: A handful of shared services at the centre, each drawn on by many ministries. Payments, mapping, and contract management are built and secured once, not reinvented in every ministry that needs them.

image.alt: A few shared service modules at the centre, each connected outward to several different government ministry buildings that all draw on the same module.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a few shared service modules grouped at the centre, each connected by clean lines outward to several different government ministry functions (firefighting, social services, grants management, registry offices, infrastructure planning), all drawing on the same central modules. A sense of build once, used by many. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.text: By fully mapping the functions of government, we can build modules which can be reused across all Ministries. Alberta owns each module, and we collapse the complexity and attack surface of our infrastructure by more than ninety percent. Created by AI, following a clear standard, these modules are easy to maintain.

blocks.title: Built once, used across government
rows: Almost every ministry takes a payment. One vetted, secure module replaces dozens of bespoke ones.
Mapping recurs across transportation, environment, land, and more. Build the map once.
A common back-office need that every ministry currently solves on its own.
A single sign-on and permission model, in place of a login screen rebuilt in every app.
The pipelines and monitoring every module needs, provided once as a platform.
One governed way to connect to the systems that remain, in place of point-to-point tangles.
blocks.caption: The shift is from a government of hundreds of bespoke systems to a government of shared, reusable modules on common compute. Each shared service is built and secured once, then drawn on by any ministry.
blocks.source: Git Insights Ministry target architecture and shared-platform design.

blocks.text: This is also where the economics come from. Because the modules are designed and built by AI under human direction, the work compresses purposefully. The entire software development lifecycle compresses. Building and testing automate heavily; the judgment calls on security, privacy, and what the public actually needs stay with people.

blocks.title: Where the work compresses
rows: About 80 percent. The AI drafts, people ratify.
About 95 percent. The bulk of the build.
About 85 percent. Tests synthesized from the code.
About 85 percent. Infrastructure written as code.
About 30 percent. Deep human engagement with stakeholders.
blocks.caption: A contingency and a program overhead are added on top, and the figure is recomputed line by line from this rubric rather than taken as a single number the AI produced. A capable 'AI harness' makes the building itself trustworthy.

blocks.text: GIM creates outputs a decision-maker can act on, not a pile of raw analysis. The dashboard presents the whole ministry as a single, evidence-backed picture, and the same source produces a plain-language executive report.

blocks.title: The GIM executive view
rows: How many legacy applications collapse into how few modern modules.
What the ministry actually does for citizens, in plain language, grounded in code.
Which capabilities are carried into the new design, and which are flagged at risk.
The effort to rebuild traditionally versus with AI, broken down by type of work.
The current vulnerabilities, sensitive-data exposure, and health, by system.
Today's point-to-point tangle set against the future shared fabric.
Where the analysis is confident, and where it openly flags gaps for a person to close.
blocks.caption: Every figure traces back to evidence in the code. The trustworthiness view is deliberate: the tool reports where it is unsure rather than presenting a uniform confidence it does not have.
blocks.source: Git Insights Ministry dashboard and brief.

blocks.title: From a tangle to a shared fabric
blocks.caption: The integration view, before and after. Today's systems connect to each other point to point, a knot no one fully holds in their head. The target connects them through one governed fabric, with shared services beneath.

image.alt: Left: a chaotic tangle of point-to-point connections between many systems. Right: the same systems connected cleanly through one central hub.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diptych. On the left, a chaotic tangle of point-to-point connecting lines between many scattered systems, a knot of wires. On the right, the same systems connected cleanly through a single central hub or fabric, orderly spokes radiating out. A sense of a knot resolved into a hub. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.text: Git Insights Ministry is the bridge from knowing the estate to rebuilding it. It turns the ground truth of [Git Insights](#/paper/bbkac) into a defensible plan: a smaller, modular, government-owned architecture, with the common parts built once for everyone. The strategies for carrying out that rebuild, from patching in place to full reconstruction, are the subject of the next paper: **[The Four Approaches to AI Modernization ](#/paper/zgym1)**.
